title: Poondi Swami
meta_description: Poondi Swamiyar was an enigmatic siddha who spent decades in villages along the River Cheyyar near Tiruvannamalai, settling permanently on the pyal of a house in Poondi village, known for his mysterious speech and psychic powers.
body_html:
"It was the fag end of 1969. On my way from Tiruvannamalai to Vellore, I stopped at Polur to have darshan at the samadhi of Saint Vitthoba. We met Duraiswami Swamigal at the mutt who introduced me to a Mr. R. Pargunam. Before I took leave of him, I asked him if he had known any living siddha.

'Oh, yes,' he said enthusiastically, 'There is a swamiyar at Poondi. A recluse, he had wandered for several years around nearby villages. Seven years ago he settled on the pyal of a house there. He has not stirred from there since.'"

No one knows Poondi Swamiyar's name or place of birth. For over three decades he was seen roaming about in the neighbouring villages around the River Cheyyar. He lived in the open — on riverbeds, under trees, in cremation grounds — regardless of weather. About seven years before this 1969 encounter he came to Poondi and sat in a small house permanently.

Poondi Swamiyar sat on the left pyal of the house — a small, tiled house on the main bus route. He held boxes of matches in a tight grip and combed his beard with his fingers. He smoked cigarettes rapidly. He accepted or refused offerings entirely according to his mood; if he accepted, it was considered a blessing in ample measure.

The Swamiyar does not reply to all questions. When he condescends to reply, some are direct answers, some are indirect references. He talks to persons at random. Most of the time he keeps a stoic silence. He looks at familiar and unfamiliar faces with equal indifference. His utterances often concern agricultural problems and village development, as well as profound spiritual truths wrapped in oblique language and proverbs.

Among notable incidents: Ponnusami Nayanar, a former Chairman of the Panchayat Board, had a stolen sugarcane grinding machine recovered after the Swamiyar told him its location; the Swamiyar appeared in a devotee's son's dream to resolve a family quarrel; his disjointed statements about the Parvata Hills turned out to be accurate descriptions of iron bars, small rails, and ladders that the author later encountered on his climb.

Poondi Swamiyar

The Poondi Swamiyar had been using the name 'Modern Theatres' (a film studio in Salem) quite often in his conversation, suggesting that part of his mysterious background was connected to that region. He once said he came to Kadaladi from Modern Theatres, that he was "dejected that he had been born without a place or name." When asked what language he had spoken to people who could not understand him, he replied, "I spoke a language they could not understand."

His parentage and place of birth and early life still remain a mystery. The author concludes: "I am convinced that this adds to the awe of his enigmatic personality."

From Six Mystics of India — By Bharanidharan (T.S. Sridhar)
notes: No hero image. Long narrative account by Bharanidharan (T.S. Sridhar). Condensed but key encounters preserved.
